author: @dondywondy
text:
I agree with @RyanJBarnard below. This is a great idea, having one side of the blade rotation turn to get little resistance while the other side presents face-on to the wind.

Now, if one used a mechanical linkage to turn the pitch-angle of the blades, as Ryan suggested, one could positively control the blade angles. A helicopter blade-pitch control uses a 'swash plate' and linkages, which seems a good fit here; and with the swash plate linked to wind direction the best face could be always presented in any wind direction.

 (Such a linkage, to turn a blade face to the wind, is an idea which I have mentally toyed with for some time myself, though in a different configuration).

Also, one could add the idea of the helicopter 'collective' control to adjust the blade speed in response to wind speed, controlling too-fast spin in high winds.

text: Two questions about this. First I am not sure 90 degrees is optimal, second it may be better to provide stops so that the vertical blade does not force the trailing blade into braking the spin.

author: @brandonkluck
text: Am I the only one thinking that a bird uses wind and works with it to glide and fly whereas if we're trying to spin a rotor wouldn't we want resistance to the wind? Don't we want the opposite of aerodynamics?

replies.author: @xrosilence.1806
replies.text: what force do you think the bird wings are leveraging to glide in the air? it's resistance. i get what you're thinking however and yes horizontally they are decreasing resistance to be dynamic but mainly vertically they are using resistance to fly. now for things like slowing  landing or turning there is some degree of horizontal resistance applied to accomplish those manoeuvres.

author: @AdamBechtol
text:
edit: I went searching for this comment 10 days later, because It was nagging me. I feel I was too brusque and harsh in  tone. It was likely an absent minded small forgetfulness on your part, and your mission isn't to direct traffic to youtube, but instead to teach and inspire invention. And when I put myself in your shoes I know I wouldn't want such hassle. I'm leaving my comment up because I think it's kind of cowardly to erase things we are embarrassed of. Like you , I just dislike waste in all its forms, including time, and like when people stop traffic in the road it makes me extra grumpy to consider the cumulative time off all the drivers behind, and in this case the many viewers. Plus I suspect I'm rather embittered by life :(. Ciao.

Psssst, Please provide the link you said that you would. 😛

For better or for worse, with your greater audience comes a greater responsibility.
When you start to add up the thousands of viewers who are spending just 30 seconds on the wild goose chase  through the comments like me to find the link you said you'd provide, that's lots of cumulative wasted time. Which you're sorta directly responsible for.
I love ya, and this channel lol, but you kinda only had one job here,  and failed. :(

If you've got members paying you $5 a month, perhaps you could use some of that money to hire a content manager. To upload things and fill out the descriptions, answer comments, etc...

I feel like you're on the verge of an exciting discovery, and taking us along for the ride is a treat. :) So i'd hate for you to lose subscribers over something so silly and little and minor as housekeeping matters. ( yet for some reason, I seem to be quite bent out of shape about it. :/ ) 
Cheers.

author: @Johnson_Rice
text:
It makes me wonder though, is there anything in nature that ACTUALLY uses wind for power/energy? Nature seems to have opted for solar and consuming fuel … (photosynthesis, or consumption of other things that at some point got their energy from photosynthesis ) 

Makes me wonder why evolution seems to have only used wind for travel (flight) and breeding (seed distribution)… 

But I can’t think of any example in nature of animals or plants that directly use only wind to energize themselves.

Where are our plants and animals that thrive from anemosynthesis?

replies.author: @mookiemorjax
replies.text:
The obvious thought that comes to mind is birds. If there weren't any wind, they'd have to work a good deal harder to fly (particularly the gliding sorta of birds). 

And if I were feeling cheeky, I'd point out that wind movement on earth is made using the sun's energy causing currents and airstreams... So ultimately even wind power is solar powered!

replies.author: @Johnson_Rice
@nugrohosumanto6390
replies.text:
Energy in organisms are based on high energy electrons and vibration of biological molecules.

That is why we have photo- and chemo-synthesis for the autotrophs. And the heterotrophs  like us make use of the energy in the C-H bonds in hydrocarbons such as carbohydrate, lipid, or protein.

The idea of energizing using wind, in my opinion, is quite improbable since wind does not energise electrons and does not vibrate molecules that much (though I can be wrong here).

A roundabout way to do it maybe if there's an organism that uses wind to generate static electricity and uses that to excite electrons in their specialized cell organelle.

I like the idea though, makes for a cool alien concept.
